section_title: Suspicious Transaction Reporting, Tipping-off, and Other Reports

content_blocks.text:
## 15. Suspicious Transaction Reporting, Tipping-off, and Other Reports

### 15.1 Suspicious Transaction Reporting

#### 15.1.1 Statutory and Regulatory Basis (Paragraph 5.5.1)

In accordance with Rule 7(2) and Rule 8(2) read with Rule 3(1)(D) of the PML Rules, and Paragraph 5.5.1 of the 2026 FIU-IND Guidelines, the Company shall promptly furnish information to FIU-IND in respect of suspicious transactions identified through transaction monitoring, including attempted suspicious transactions.

This obligation extends to:

(a) Completed transactions identified as suspicious;

(b) Attempted transactions identified as suspicious (whether or not the transaction was ultimately completed);

(c) Series of transactions that, considered together, give rise to a reasonable ground of suspicion.

#### 15.1.2 Alert Generation Framework (Paragraph 5.5.2)

An alert serves as the initial step in detecting a suspicious transaction, and acts as a red flag triggered by potentially suspicious activity.

The Company shall put in place robust systems and processes to generate alerts based on predefined red-flag indicators (RFIs). The principles for implementing RFIs, along with common minimum alerts and parameters for generating these alerts, shall be in line with:

(a) The Guidances issued by FIU-IND for detecting STRs from time to time, under Rule 7(3) of the PML Rules;

(b) Additional indicators identified by the Company through its own risk assessment under Section 8.1 of this Policy;

(c) Typologies, red-flag indicators, and instructions issued by FIU-IND from time to time.

#### 15.1.6 Internal Workflow for STR Identification, Escalation, and Filing

The Company shall implement and maintain a documented internal workflow for the identification, escalation, drafting, review, approval, filing, and post-filing record-keeping of STRs. The workflow shall, at a minimum, comprise the following stages:

**(a) Detection:**

Alerts generated by transaction monitoring (Section 12.2 of this Policy), sanctions screening (Section 14 of this Policy), CDD review (Sections 10 and 11 of this Policy), and any other source (including manual escalations from staff, customer service teams, blockchain analytics vendors, or law enforcement requests) shall be logged in the Company's case management system.

**(b) Initial Triage:**

The AML/CFT/CPF compliance team shall conduct initial triage of each alert to determine whether further investigation is warranted.

**(c) Investigation:**

Where further investigation is warranted, the investigating compliance officer shall:

(i) Gather all relevant information (KYC, transaction history, blockchain analytics, public information about the client, counterparties, and wallet addresses);

(ii) Document the investigation steps, findings, and rationale;

(iii) Determine whether reasonable grounds for suspicion exist.

**(d) Escalation to Principal Officer:**

Where reasonable grounds for suspicion are identified, the case shall be escalated to the Principal Officer with a recommendation on whether to file an STR.

**(e) Principal Officer Decision (Paragraphs 3.3(e), 3.3(f), 3.3(g)):**

The ultimate analysis and decision-making on STR filing rests with the Principal Officer. The Principal Officer shall:

(i) Review the investigation report and recommendation;

(ii) Record reasons for treating any transaction or series of transactions as suspicious;

(iii) Where the decision is not to file an STR, clearly record the reasons for closure of the alert;

(iv) Where the decision is to file an STR, approve drafting and filing;

(v) Ensure no undue delay in arriving at the conclusion once a suspicious transaction is escalated.

**(f) Drafting:**

The AML/CFT/CPF compliance team shall draft the STR in the format prescribed by FIU-IND, ensuring inclusion of all elements specified in Section 15.1.3 above and the quality standards specified in Section 15.1.4.

**(g) Final Review:**

The Principal Officer shall conduct final review of the STR before filing, with particular attention to the grounds of suspicion, completeness of supporting information, and accuracy of all factual details.

**(h) Filing:**

The approved STR shall be filed promptly with FIU-IND via the FINGate portal or other prescribed mechanism.

**(i) Post-Filing Record-Keeping:**

The Company shall retain, for a minimum period of five (5) years from the date of filing (or such longer period as may be required by FIU-IND or by applicable law):

(i) The STR itself;

(ii) The underlying investigation file;

(iii) All supporting documentation, communications, and decisional records;

(iv) Filing acknowledgements from FIU-IND.

Record-keeping shall comply with the broader requirements set out in Section 16 of this Policy.

**(j) Continued Monitoring:**

Filing of an STR does not, by itself, require closure or termination of the client relationship. The Company shall continue monitoring the client and the relevant transactions, and may file follow-up or supplementary STRs if additional suspicious activity is identified. Termination of the relationship may be appropriate where enhanced measures under CDD cannot be applied or where the relationship otherwise falls outside the Company's risk appetite, in accordance with Sections 7 and 11.2 of this Policy.

### 15.3 Other Reports to FIU-IND

#### 15.3.1 Monthly Report (Paragraph 5.7)

In accordance with Paragraph 5.7 of the 2026 FIU-IND Guidelines, the Company shall furnish a Monthly Report to FIU-IND in such form, time, and manner as may be prescribed by FIU-IND from time to time.

The Monthly Report shall include:

(a) Essential metrics relating to the Company's operations as a VDA SP;

(b) Activity indicators (transaction volumes, customer counts, geographic distribution, and similar metrics);

(c) Compliance status, including any material developments;

(d) Other relevant details as deemed necessary by FIU-IND from time to time.

The Monthly Report shall be in a consolidated format as prescribed by FIU-IND. The Principal Officer shall be responsible for ensuring timely and accurate filing.

#### 15.3.2 Other Prescribed Reports

In addition to STRs and the Monthly Report, the Company shall file all other reports prescribed under Rule 3 of the PML Rules and other applicable rules and circulars, where applicable to the Company's activities, including:

(a) **Cash Transaction Reports (CTRs):** Where the Company handles cash transactions exceeding prescribed thresholds. (For most pure VDA SP operations, CTRs may not be operationally relevant, but where the Company's business model includes cash handling above thresholds, CTRs shall be filed.)

(b) **Non-Profit Organisation Transaction Reports (NTRs):** For transactions involving Non-Profit Organisations where reporting thresholds and conditions are met under Rule 3 of the PML Rules.

(c) **Cross-Border Wire Transfer Reports:** Where applicable based on the Company's cross-border transaction activity.

(d) **Counterfeit Currency Reports (CCRs):** Where applicable.

(e) **Any additional reports** prescribed by FIU-IND for VDA SPs from time to time.

#### 15.3.3 Responses to Section 12A Information Requests

In accordance with Section 12A of the PMLA and Paragraph 3.4.3(f) of the 2026 Guidelines, the Company shall maintain an internal mechanism for furnishing records and information to FIU-IND within such time and in such manner as desired by FIU-IND under Section 12A of the PMLA.

The Principal Officer shall coordinate the response to all Section 12A requests, ensuring:

(a) Receipt of the request is acknowledged within the timeframe specified;

(b) The requested records and information are gathered from relevant business and technology functions;

(c) Information is provided within the timeframe specified in the request;

(d) Records of all Section 12A requests, the responses provided, and the supporting documentation are retained in accordance with Section 16 of this Policy.
